skill.name: graph-interpretation
skill.description: Use when interpreting scientific graphs and charts, explaining data visualizations for research presentations, writing figure captions for publications, or analyzing trends in clinical research data. Converts complex visual data into clear, accurate explanations for academic papers, clinical reports, and public presentations.
skill.summary: Interprets scientific graphs and generates publication-ready figure captions, statistical summaries, and audience-specific explanations for academic and clinical research.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 71 potential issues across 5 files (756 lines), producing a risk score of 100/100. All 71 findings were evaluated and confirmed as false positives. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' alerts were triggered by the substring 'des' appearing in words like 'description' and 'describe' — no actual cryptographic code exists in any file. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' alerts were triggered by standard Markdown code fences (```bash, ```python) and inline backticks used for documentation formatting — no shell execution occurs. 'System reconnaissance' alerts matched on words like 'audit' and 'validation' in evaluation artifacts. The 'hardcoded URL' alert is a documentation link to the GitHub repository. The bundled Python script (scripts/main.py) is a simple template-based description generator with no network access, no cryptographic operations, no command injection vectors, and no file system writes. The skill does legitimately reference an executable script path and a source URL, warranting a low risk classification rather than safe, but no malicious behavior was detected.

low_findings.title: Executable script path in documentation
low_findings.description: The skill documentation instructs users to run 'python scripts/main.py' and other Python commands. The bundled script is safe (template-based text generation only), but any skill that guides command execution carries inherent risk if the script were modified in a future update.

low_findings.title: Source URL reference in markdown
low_findings.description: The skill file contains a hyperlink to the GitHub repository for attribution. This is a documentation reference, not a programmatic network request, and poses no data exfiltration risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: DES substring false positive in description words
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 'des' substring in words like 'description', 'describe', and 'describing' as the DES weak encryption algorithm. No cryptographic code exists in the skill. This affected 26 locations across all 5 files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown code fences flagged as shell execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged Markdown code blocks (```bash, ```python, ```text) and inline backticks as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are standard Markdown formatting elements used for documenting code examples and CLI commands, not actual shell execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Audit terminology flagged as system reconnaissance
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged words like 'audit', 'validation', and 'check' in the evaluation result JSON and skill documentation as system reconnaissance. These are standard evaluation and quality assurance terms, not reconnaissance commands.

content.user_title: Interpret Scientific Graphs with AI-Powered Analysis
content.value_statement: Researchers struggle to interpret complex scientific charts and write publication-ready figure captions. This skill provides structured graph analysis, statistical interpretation, and audience-specific explanations for academic and clinical communications.
actual_capabilities: Interpret multiple graph types including Kaplan-Meier curves, forest plots, ROC curves, box plots, scatter plots, bar charts, heatmaps, and volcano plots
Extract and report statistical measures with proper formatting for p-values, confidence intervals, and effect sizes
Generate audience-specific explanations tailored for researchers, clinicians, patients, and policy makers
Create publication-ready figure captions in journal, presentation, or poster styles with appropriate statistical detail
Perform critical appraisal of graph quality including axis scaling checks, error bar verification, and sample size assessment
Apply statistical reporting standards to produce consistent, reproducible output across research documents
limitations: Cannot visually analyze actual image files — requires graph data to be described in text form by the user
Output quality depends on the completeness and accuracy of input data provided by the user
Does not perform original statistical calculations — interprets and reports statistics already present in source materials
Limited to the graph types documented in the skill references and may not cover specialized or novel visualization formats

use_cases.title: Clinical Research Reporting
use_cases.description: Interpret survival curves, forest plots, and diagnostic test results for clinical trial publications. Generate audience-appropriate explanations for regulatory submissions and peer-reviewed journals.

use_cases.title: Academic Publication Support
use_cases.description: Write journal-compliant figure captions and statistical interpretations for research papers. Apply reporting standards for reproducible scientific communication across disciplines.

use_cases.title: Public Health Communication
use_cases.description: Translate complex scientific data into plain-language explanations for policy briefs, patient materials, and public presentations while maintaining accuracy.
use_cases.target_user: Science communicators and public health professionals

prompt_templates.title: Basic Graph Description
prompt_templates.prompt: Describe the key findings from this {graph_type} showing {variable} across {groups}. Include the main trend, any notable differences between groups, and one limitation of the visualization.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick summary when preparing presentation slides or meeting notes

prompt_templates.title: Publication Figure Caption
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a figure caption for a {graph_type} in {journal} style. The graph shows {data_description}. Include the statistical test used, sample size, and key result with confidence intervals. Keep it under {word_count} words.
prompt_templates.scenario: Preparing figures for journal submission with specific formatting requirements

prompt_templates.title: Audience-Specific Explanation
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the clinical significance of this {graph_type} showing {finding} to an audience of {audience_type}. Use terminology appropriate for their background and highlight what this means for patient care or research direction.
prompt_templates.scenario: Communicating the same results to different stakeholders with varying expertise

prompt_templates.title: Critical Graph Appraisal
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this {graph_type} for potential issues in scientific communication. Check axis scaling, error bar representation, sample size adequacy, and whether the graph type matches the data. List any concerns and suggest specific improvements.
prompt_templates.scenario: Peer review preparation or quality control of scientific figures before submission

output_examples.input: Describe a Kaplan-Meier survival curve showing improved overall survival with experimental treatment in a phase 3 oncology trial
output_examples.output: Figure 1. Kaplan-Meier estimate of overall survival. The x-axis shows time in months and the y-axis shows the proportion of patients surviving. The experimental arm (solid line) demonstrated superior survival compared to the control arm (dashed line). Median overall survival was 19.6 months for the experimental arm versus 14.2 months for the control arm (HR 0.72, 95% CI 0.58-0.89, p=0.003). This represents a 32% reduction in the risk of death. The proportional hazards assumption was verified (p=0.42). Separation of the curves occurred early and was sustained throughout follow-up, suggesting consistent treatment benefit.

output_examples.input: Explain a forest plot from a meta-analysis of five studies examining treatment effect on cardiovascular outcomes
output_examples.output: The forest plot summarizes treatment effects across five studies examining cardiovascular outcomes. Each horizontal line represents the 95% confidence interval for an individual study, with the square size proportional to the study weight in the meta-analysis. The overall pooled effect (diamond) shows a statistically significant benefit for the treatment (HR 0.82, 95% CI 0.71-0.95). Moderate between-study heterogeneity was observed (I-squared equals 45%, p equals 0.12), indicating some variability in treatment effects across studies. The effect direction favors the treatment in all five studies, though Study C was not individually statistically significant as its confidence interval crossed 1.0.

best_practices: Provide specific data values and graph context before requesting interpretation to receive the most accurate and detailed results
Specify your target audience and desired output format early so explanations use appropriate terminology and depth for each reader group
Review all generated interpretations for accuracy against your source data before including them in publications or presentations
anti_patterns: Do not ask the skill to generate or fabricate data values — it interprets only the data you provide and will refuse to invent results
Do not rely on generated interpretations as medical advice, diagnostic guidance, or treatment recommendations — outputs are for academic communication only
Do not skip specifying the graph type and data context — interpretation quality depends directly on knowing the visualization format and study design

faq.question: What types of scientific graphs can this skill interpret?
faq.answer: The skill supports Kaplan-Meier survival curves, forest plots, ROC curves, box plots, scatter plots, bar charts, heatmaps, volcano plots, and other common scientific visualization formats used in clinical and academic research.

faq.question: Does this skill analyze actual image files or screenshots of graphs?
faq.answer: No. You must describe the data shown in the graph using text. The skill interprets the data you provide in written form rather than analyzing visual image content directly.

faq.question: Can this skill perform statistical calculations on raw data?
faq.answer: No. The skill interprets and reports statistics from your input data but does not run independent statistical tests or generate new analyses from raw datasets.

faq.question: What audience types are supported for tailored explanations?
faq.answer: The skill can tailor the complexity and terminology of explanations for researchers, clinicians, patients, policy makers, and general audiences.

faq.question: Is this skill suitable for clinical decision-making or diagnostic support?
faq.answer: No. This skill supports academic writing and educational communication only. It should never be used for diagnostic decisions, treatment planning, or patient-specific clinical guidance.

faq.question: What format options are available for figure caption generation?
faq.answer: The skill generates captions in journal, presentation, and poster styles. Verify the output against your specific target publication guidelines as formatting requirements vary between journals.
